coredata.dc:title: Betel leaf derived multicolor emitting carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for imaging mouse normal fibroblast and human thyroid cancer cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Gangadaran, Prakash
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Perumal, Suguna
Sundramoorthy, Ashok K.
Vinodh, Rajangam
Rajendran, Ramya Lakshmi
Ahn, Byeong-Cheol
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: A facile and eco-friendly approach was adapted for the preparation of multicolor emitting carbon dots (BL-MECDs) by hydrothermal method using betel leaves. BL-MECDs were synthesized at various reaction times-12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h at 200 °C. The reaction times were varied to find the optimum reaction condition to obtain BL-MECDs with excellent optical- and chemical properties. BL-MECDs were characterized by various analytical techniques. The surface morphology, degree of graphitization, and their composition were examined by field emission scanning electron microscope, Raman spectrum, and attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, respectively. The optical properties were evaluated via ultraviolet–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The result suggests that BL-MECDs prepared at 36 h have enriched functional groups and excellent fluorescence emission with acceptable quantum yield (15%). Thus, BL-MECDs prepared at 36 h were utilized for further studies. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy confirmed the existence of functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups. Moreover, the obtained BL-MECDs exhibited excellent prolonged stability and photostability even after 150 days of storage in atmospheric condition and by continuous irradiation of about 100 min with 365 nm UV-lamb, respectively. Further, BL-MECDs showed excellent biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity and thus explored for cell imaging for normal mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) and human anaplastic thyroid cancer cells line (SW1736). Cellular fluorescence imaging suggesting that BL-MECDs have great potential in bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Betel leaf
Multicolor emitting carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and optical measurements of synthesized BL-MECDs
Normal and cancer cellular viability to synthesized BL-MECDs
Multicolor imaging of normal and cancer cells using synthesized BL-MECDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government MSIT ( 2021R1A2B5B02002436 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FE-SEM image and corresponding elemental mapping of (b) carbon, (c) oxygen, (d) nitrogen, and (e) overlapping of BL-MECDs at hydrothermal time of 12 h. (f) FE-SEM image and corresponding elemental mapping of (g) carbon, (h) oxygen, (i) nitrogen, and (j) overlapping of BL-MECDs at hydrothermal time of 24 h.

(a) FE-SEM image and corresponding elemental mapping of (b) carbon, (c) oxygen, (d) nitrogen, and (e) overlapping of BL-MECDs at hydrothermal time of 36 h. (f) FE-SEM image and corresponding elemental mapping of (g) carbon, (h) oxygen, (i) nitrogen, and (j) overlapping of BL-MECDs at hydrothermal time of 48 h.
(a) Raman spectra and (b) ATR-FTIR spectra of BL-MECDs with different hydrothermal times (12, 24, 36, and 48 h).

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of BL-MECDs at different hydrothermal times. Fluorescence emission spectra of BL-MECDs at different hydrothermal times (b) 12 h, (c) 24 h, (d) 36 h, and (e) 48 h. (e) The fluorescence spectra of BL-MECDs maximum emission at different hydrothermal times (12–48 h) by the excitation of 340 nm and inset (e) digital photographs of aqueous BL-MECDs at different hydrothermal times from 12 to 48 h under the 365 nm UV-light.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of BL-MECDs at hydrothermal time of 36 h. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s level, (c) N 1s level, and (d) O 1s level of the BL-MECDs at a hydrothermal time of 36 h.
(a and b) HRTEM images with different magnifications of BL-MECDs at hydrothermal time of 36 h.

Cytotoxicity of the synthesized BL-MECDs to normal and cancer cells. (a) The histogram shows cellular viability of L929 (Mouse fibroblast cell lines) as determined by CCK8 assay 24 h after treatment with 0–40 μg/mL of BL-MECDs; (n = 7). (b) The histogram shows the cellular viability of SW1736 (human anaplastic thyroid cancer cells line) as determined by CCK8 assay 24 h after treatment with 0–40 μg/mL of BL-MECDs; (n = 7). The mean and SD of the experiment are plotted. *p < 0.05. The student's t-test was used.

Fluorescent imaging of normal and cancer cells labeled with BL-MECDs. (a) Laser Confocal images of L929 cells (Mouse fibroblast cell lines) were incubated with 30 μg/mL of CDs for 24 h. (b) Laser Confocal images of SW1736 cells (human anaplastic thyroid cancer cells line) incubated with 30 μg/mL of CDs for 24 h. Scale bar: 20 μm.
Illustration of synthesis of BL-MECDs from betel leaves by hydrothermal method.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI): Materials, instrumentation methods, quantum yield measurement, stability measurements, EDX spectra, deconvoluted Raman spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra, and digital fluorescence photographs at different hydrothermal times of the betel leaf derived multicolor emitting carbon dots (BL-MECDs).

head.ce:title: Betel leaf derived multicolor emitting carbon dots as a fluorescent probe for imaging mouse normal fibroblast and human thyroid cancer cells

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and eco-friendly approach was adapted for the preparation of multicolor emitting carbon dots (BL-MECDs) by hydrothermal method using betel leaves. BL-MECDs were synthesized at various reaction times-12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h at 200 °C. The reaction times were varied to find the optimum reaction condition to obtain BL-MECDs with excellent optical- and chemical properties. BL-MECDs were characterized by various analytical techniques. The surface morphology, degree of graphitization, and their composition were examined by field emission scanning electron microscope, Raman spectrum, and attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, respectively. The optical properties were evaluated via ultraviolet–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The result suggests that BL-MECDs prepared at 36 h have enriched functional groups and excellent fluorescence emission with acceptable quantum yield (15%). Thus, BL-MECDs prepared at 36 h were utilized for further studies. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy confirmed the existence of functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups. Moreover, the obtained BL-MECDs exhibited excellent prolonged stability and photostability even after 150 days of storage in atmospheric condition and by continuous irradiation of about 100 min with 365 nm UV-lamb, respectively. Further, BL-MECDs showed excellent biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity and thus explored for cell imaging for normal mouse fibroblast cell line (L929) and human anaplastic thyroid cancer cells line (SW1736). Cellular fluorescence imaging suggesting that BL-MECDs have great potential in bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon dots (CDs) preparation by eco-friendly methods was studied extensively. CDs are carbon-based materials that are spherical with a size range below 10 nm [1–3]. CDs have excellent properties such as good solubility or dispersibility in water [4], chemical stability [5], photostability [6], and low toxicity [7,8]. Thus CDs find application in a wide range such as sensors [4], catalyst [9], in bio-application such as bioimaging [6,10,11], and as drug carriers [12,13]. CDs are prepared by top-down and bottom-up approaches that include such as arc discharge [14], combustion [15], laser ablation [10], hydrothermal methods, and so on were exist. Among these methods, researchers considered the hydrothermal method for the preparation of CDs because it is a simple, direct, and efficient method [16]. Superior to biocompatibility and environmentally friendly, many functional groups exist on the surface of the CDs. Functional groups on the surface were from the plant constituents or by surface passivation. CDs prepared using plant sources such as orange juice [1], coriander leaves [17], onion [18], grape juice [19] and etc [20–22] endows optical properties such as high quantum yield (QY). Further to improve the optical properties, CDs are doped with other elements such as nitrogen and sulfur atoms [23].

Photoluminescent in CDs induced by quantum size effect [24], edge effects [25], surface states [26], molecular state [27] and functional groups [28]. The improved optical properties of CDs pave the wave for biological applications such as bioimaging/bio labeling. CDs possess exceptional properties, such as superior photostability [29], high brightness [30], biocompatibility, and spontaneous penetration capabilities into cells [31]. CDs are reported as a potential candidate for imaging and monitoring of cells in biomedical applications [32].

In this experiment, using betel leaves (BLs), multicolor emitting carbon dots (MECDs) were synthesized. The BLs are easily available and affordable as a common dessert. The minerals, vitamin C, calcium, carotene, niacin, riboflavin, thiamine, tannin, sugar, and diastase as well as an essential oil are rich in betel leaves. Betel leaves have been demonstrated as a potential source of bioactive compounds that have many medicinal properties. Therefore, the betel leaves were consumed as both carbon and nitrogen source for the synthesis of durable fluorescence BL-MECDs. The BL-MECDs were synthesized at different reaction times by an environment-friendly hydrothermal method. The effect of reaction times during the synthesize of BL-MECDs was effectively studied to find optimum reaction conditions to obtain BL-MECDs with excellent optical properties. The properties such as good dispersibility in water, acceptable QY, and the biocompatibility of BL-MECDs allowed us to use them in bioimaging applications. BL-MECDs were used for imaging normal mouse fibroblast cell lines (L929) and human anaplastic thyroid cancer cells lines (SW1736) in vitro and they showed excellent biocompatibility with low toxicity to both cells. The BL-MECDs were effectively internalized into the L929 and SW1736 that emitted a strong fluorescent signal. Multi-color cellular fluorescence imaging suggesting BL-MECDs as a new potential candidate for bioimaging applications. Furthermore, only 30 μg/mL of the BL-MECDs was used for bioimaging purposes, suggesting that BL-MECDs can be utilized as potent in vitro fluorescent probes for the detection of human colon cancer cell infections in the biological system. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the earliest report on imaging of L929 and SW1736 using CDs from bio-source.

CDs from betel leaves were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Typically, betel leaves were washed with DI water, cut into small pieces, and blended in a mixer. The blended mixture was transferred to an autoclave container and hydrothermal treatment was carried out at 200 °C by varying the reaction time as 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h. After the desired reaction time, the mixture was cooled naturally to RT and filtered using a mixed cellulose ester filter with 0.22 μm pore size. The obtained brown-colored solution emitted bright blue/cyan fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. A portion was stored for further use and by drying the remaining solution using a freeze dryer a brown solid (BL-MECDs) was obtained and utilized for structural analyses.

The mouse fibroblast cell line, L929 (L cell, L-929, a derivative of Strain L) was generously gifted by Dr. Yong Hyun Jeon (Daegu-Gyeongbuk Medical Innovation Foundation, Daegu, Republic of Korea). Human anaplastic thyroid cancer cell line SW1736 was generously gifted by Dr. Minho Shong (School of Medicine, Chungnam National University). L929 cells were maintained in Roswell Park Memorial Institute-1640 medium (HyClone, Logan, UT, USA) and SW1736 cells were maintained in Roswell Park Memorial Institute-1640 medium (HyClone). Both the mediums were supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (HyClone) and 1% Cell counting kit 8 (Gibco, Carlsbad, CA, USA) at 37 °C with 5% CO2.

L929 or SW1736 cells (10,000/well) were seeded into a 96-well plate in 100 μL of complete medium overnight under a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. Cells were treated with different concentrations of BL-MECDs (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, and 40 μg/mL) for 24 h 10 μL of CCK8 solution (Dojindo Molecular Technologies, MD, USA) was added to each well. Cells were incubated under a 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, optical density (OD) was measured on an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay plate reader according to the manufacturer's protocol.

L929 or SW1736 cells (5000/well) were seeded into 4 well slide-chamber (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Republic of Korea) in 500 μL of complete medium overnight under 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. Cells were treated with BL-MECDs (30 μg/mL) for 24 h. After, 24 h, cells were washed twice PBS for 10 min. Then, cells were fixed with 2% PFA for 10 min at room temperature. Then, cells were washed twice PBS for 10 min. Slides were mounted with an anti-fade mounting medium with DAPI (Vectashield, CA, USA). Cells were imaged under the highly sensitive super-resolution confocal laser scanning microscope LSM 800 (Carl Zeiss, Baden-Württemberg, Germany) for blue (Excitation: 401; Emission: 422 and Detection: 400–505), green (Excitation: 493; Emission: 517 and Detection: 450–550) and red (Excitation: 553; Emission: 568 and Detection: 400–700).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical measurements of synthesized BL-MECDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The surface morphology of the synthesized BL-MECDs at various hydrothermal reaction times was examined by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM). Figs. 1 and 2
show the FE-SEM images of BL-MECDs prepared for 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h reaction times. Irregular shapes of BL-MECDs are observed regardless of reaction time. However, elemental mapping measurements reveal distinct changes in the reaction time. The intensities of oxygen (O), carbon (C), and nitrogen (N) atoms are increased with reaction time. Further, the elemental composition of BL-MECDs prepared for various reaction times-12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h was examined with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy analyzer (Fig. S1(a-d)). The existence of O and N is due to the oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface and or edges of the BL-MECDs. EDX analysis (Fig. S1) shows elemental atomic percentage as 12 h (C = 79%, N = 2% and O = 19%), 24 h (C = 76%, N = 3% and O = 21%), 36 h (C = 67%, N = 5% and O = 28%), and 48 h (C = 66%, N = 5% and O = 29%). The additional peaks of platinum (Pt) and silicon (Si) are due to the Pt coating over the sample and silicon wafer that is used as a substrate, respectively. This discloses that the hydrothermal treatment of betel leaves for a longer reaction time helps in occurrences of the functional groups with higher concentrations. Particularly, intensities of O and N atoms show a significant increase with reaction time. However, intensities of O and N atoms for BL-MECDs prepared in 36 h and 48 h appears the same. This advises that the 36 h is an optimum reaction condition to prepare BL-MECDs with enriched functional groups.

The formation of BL-MECDs composite is further evidenced by the corresponding Raman spectra. Fig. 3 (a) shows the Raman spectra of BL-MECDs prepared in 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h. BL-MECDs exhibits two characteristic peaks D and G bands: the D-band at ∼1350 cm−1, arising from defects in the graphitic lattice, sp3 carbon, and abundant functional groups on the surfaces [33,34]. The G-band at ∼1580 cm−1 is related to the sp2 carbon lattice (graphitic lattice) [35,36]. The relative intensities of D-band and G-band (ID/IG) for BL-MECDs prepared in 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h were measured as 0.80, 0.78, 0.85, and 0.83, respectively. Generally, the presence of D- and G-band reveal the existence of disordered carbon in the BL-MECDs, these defects are due to the functional groups. D-band and G-band of BL-MECDs are not separated clearly; hence, we cannot strongly conclude the degree of graphitization. Thus, the Raman spectra were deconvoluted using Casa XPS software. The integral area ratios of D-band to G-band (AD/AG) were calculated as 2.3, 2.1, 2.8, and 2.6 for 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h, respectively (Fig. S2 (a-d)). The AD/AG value decreased until 24 h, rapidly increased at 36 h, and again slightly decreased at 48 h. The higher value of AD/AG at 36 h than compared to other reaction times suggesting the defects of carbon structure which are due to the presence of abundant functional groups on the BL-MECDs. In addition, the integral area of the D-band is dominating the G-band with reaction time from 12 to 48 h because of the functional groups on the surface and or on the edges of the BL-MECDs. Furthermore, the intensities of the D- and G-bands increased gradually with the reaction time from 12 to 48 h. This corresponds to the defects of graphitic structure, which increases with the reaction time. Nearly similar results were obtained at 36 and 48 h of reaction time; hence, we concluded that the 36 h was an optimum for the better formation of BL-MECDs with rich functionalities. Moreover, the Raman studies show consistent results with FE-SEM measurements. Attenuated total reflection-Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) was used to gain insight into the chemical composition of BL-MECDs over the reaction time (Fig. 3 (b)). A strong/broad absorption peaks between 3200 and 3450 cm−1 are ascribed to the presence of –NH and –OH stretching [37,38]. C–H asymmetric and symmetric stretching was observed at 2932 and 2865 cm−1, respectively [39], the peaks at 1680 and 1588 cm−1 correspond to the distinctive peaks of CO and CC groups in BL-MECDs, respectively [40–42]. The peaks between 1460 and 1350 cm−1 attribute to C–N stretching and O–H/C–H bending vibrations. The peaks in the region 1290-750 cm−1 are ascribed to C–OH stretching, C–O/C–O–C (epoxy) functional groups, and out plane wagging of the –CH in the aromatic ring [43]. ATR-FTIR reveals the existence of –COOH, C–O/C–O–C, CC, C–H, and C–N functional groups in BL-MECDs. The functional groups in BL-MECDs composite were found to be the same regardless of reaction times. Significantly, the absorption intensities of BL-MECDs composites prepared in 36 h are high than compared to the intensities of composites prepared in 12, 24, and 48 h. This suggests that 36 h reaction time is an optimum condition to prepare BL-MECDs composites with enriched functional groups. FE-SEM, Raman, and ATR-FTIR studies revealed that 36 h reaction time is an optimum condition to prepare functional group enriched BL-MECDs.

To evaluate the optical properties of the synthesized BL-MECDs, Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorbance was measured using their water dispersions. UV–vis absorption spectra of BL-MECDs in different reaction times 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h are illustrated in Fig. 4 (a). Fig. 4 (a) reveal two absorption bands at 275 nm and 322 nm which were ascribed to the π→π* (presence of aromatic π orbitals of sp2 hybridized CC bonds in the core) and non-conjugated n→π* transition (CO functional groups at the surface of BL-MECDs), respectively [44–46]. The long tail up to 510 nm is due to low energy transitions within surface states which were formed by the surface functional groups [47]. At the same concentration and environmental conditions, the intensity of BL-MECDs prepared for 12 h, is low than the BL-MECDs composites prepared in 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h. This suggests that the optical properties of BL-MECDs are dependent on the reaction time. Reaction-dependent optical properties were further confirmed from the fluorescence spectral studies. The fluorescence excitation spectra (Fig. S3) advise the maximum around 340 nm. To confirm, whether the prepared BL-MECDs show excitation-dependent fluorescence emission or not, the emission spectra were traced on varying excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 420 nm. As revealed in Fig. 4 (b-e), the fluorescence spectra of BL-MECDs displayed highly dependent fluorescent emission spectra with the excitation wavelength. Initially, when the excitation increased up to 340 nm, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased and the maximum intensity was obtained at 340 nm regardless of the reaction time. However, with the further increase in excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 420 nm, the emission intensity decreased gradually as shown in Fig. 4 (b–e), regardless of the reaction time. In addition, the emission peak position shifts to longer wavelengths (red-shift) with an increase in the excitation wavelength. The actual mechanism for fluorescence is still not clear but there are reports about the role of the surface states [48–50] that exist on the BL-MECDs surface. These surface states correspond to different functional groups on the surface. When the BL-MECDs are irradiated, these surface states trap excitons, and the energy is released in the form of photons. Thus, fluorescence emission may be attributed to the radiative recombination of excitons in the surface states which act as emission centers. The corresponding emission centers would dominate at different excitation wavelengths, resulting in excitation-dependent fluorescence emission [48–50]. Interestingly, it is observed that the maximum intensity at 340 nm shows a distinct difference in the intensity with reaction time (Fig. 4 (f)). The difference in intensity for BL-MECDs prepared for 24 h showed 1.6 times higher and BL-MECDs prepared for 36 h and 48 h showed nearly 2.5 times higher intensity than compared to BL-MECDs prepared for 12 h. However, composites BL-MECDs prepared for 36 h and 48 h, do not show significant differences in the fluorescence intensities. Further, the QYs of BL-MECDs at different reaction times were calculated using quinine sulfate as a standard reference (Equation (S1)). The QY with reaction time was calculated as 7, 10, 15, and 15.5% for 12, 24, 36, and 48 h, respectively. This suggests that the fluorescence properties are improved with the reaction time, which is because of the increase in functional groups. Further, the fluorescence of BL-MECDs at different hydrothermal times was observed by fluorescence photography. The aqueous BL-MECDs prepared at different hydrothermal times were illuminated under the daylight (Fig. S4) and 365 nm UV-light (inset Fig. 4 (f)). In the pictorial representation, all the samples display the pale yellow color (Fig. S4) and bright cyan/blue color (inset Fig 4 (f) & Fig. S4) in the presence of daylight and UV light by naked eye views, respectively. The brightness of fluorescence gradually increasing with increasing the reaction time from 12 to 48 h that can see vividly by naked eyes. Moreover, the synthesized BL-MECDs emit bright fluorescence without any surface functionalization and or surface treatment.

Since reaction time 36 h, is considered as an optimum reaction condition to obtain BL-MECDs with enriched functional groups. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurement was done only for BL-MECDs composite prepared in 36 h. XPS studies were done to evident the components of the surface on BL-MECDs. Fig. 5 (a) shows a survey spectrum of BL-MECDs prepared in 36 h, three major peaks around 284, 400, and 533 eV are ascribed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s with the atomic percentages of 70, 5, and 25, respectively. The deconvoluted high-resolution C 1s spectra in Fig. 5 (b) show four peaks at 284.7, 285.7, 286.6, and 288.2 eV can be assigned to CC/C–C, C–OH, C–N–C/C–O–C, OC–OH groups, respectively [51,52]. As shown in Fig. 5 (c) deconvoluted high-resolution N 1s exhibit three peaks at 398.8, 400.0, and 401.5 eV corresponding to C–N–C (pyridinic N), C–N–H (pyrrolic N), and (C)3–N (graphitic N), respectively [53,54]. The deconvoluted high-resolution spectra of O 1s (Fig. 5 (d)) showed four distinct peaks at 531.0, 531.9, 532.8, and 533.6 eV attributed to –CO, -C-OH, -C-O-C, and OC–OH, respectively [38,55,56]. XPS demonstrated that the surface of BL-MECDs is enriched with nitrogen, carbonyl, and hydroxyl functionalized groups. These XPS results are consistent with Raman, ATR-FTIR, and FE-SEM results. Since 36 h reaction time confirmed the functional groups enriched BL-MECDs, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) measurement was carried out to study the surface morphology. Fig. 6 shows HR-TEM images of BL-MECDs with different magnifications. Fig. 6 (a) reveals the spherical shape particles of BL-MECDs with an average particle size of 4.5 ± 0.5 nm. Fig. 6 (b) indicating that BL-MECDs lattice fringe image, d-spacing was calculated as 0.21 nm which show consistent with the (100) plane spacing of sp2 carbon [57]. The lattice fringes within the core of the particles with an amorphous outer shell that resulting in the as-synthesized whole BL-MECDs have an acceptable crystalline carbon structure. In general, CDs from natural carbon sources will have an amorphous structure or will have a crystalline core with an amorphous outer shell [58].

The photostability and prolonged stability of the BL-MECDs are very important for their practical applications including biological imaging [59]. To study the prolonging stability of BL-MECDs, the synthesized aqueous BL-MECDs solution was stored for several days/months, and the fluorescence properties are shown as Fig. S5 (a). In the spectra (Fig. S5 (a)), insignificant changes in the fluorescence emission intensity even after 150 days of storage without shift were observed. On the other hand, the 365 nm UV light continuously shined over the synthesized aqueous BL-MECDs solution for 100 min, and the fluorescence emission at different time intervals was measured to determine the photostability of BL-MECDs. The fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. S5 (b)) reveals the insignificant changes in the intensity and the position was not shifted with irradiation time. These observations revealed that the synthesized BL-MECDs have excellent prolonged and photostability properties. The hydrophilicity and bright fluorescence suggest the use of BL-MECDs in various nano-biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Normal and cancer cellular viability to synthesized BL-MECDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity of the synthesized BL-MECDs was investigated on normal and cancer cell lines: L929 and SW1736. After 24 h of post-treatment with BL-MECDs in 0–40 μg/mL, L929 cells showed excellent viability up to 35 μg/mL concentration with 96.31% and whereas, 40 μg/mL showed a significant (p < 0.05) cytotoxicity with the cell viability of 93.27% (Fig. 7 (a)). The SW1736 cells showed excellent viability for all the concentrations (0–40 μg/mL) and no significant (p > 0.05) cytotoxicity with cell viability of around 100% was observed (Fig. 7 (b)). These results suggest that synthesized BL-MECDs are low-toxic to mouse fibroblast cell lines and non-toxic to human anaplastic thyroid cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor imaging of normal and cancer cells using synthesized BL-MECDs
ce:section.ce:para: As the synthesized BL-MECDs showed excellent biocompatibility to normal and cancer cells, they were subjected to in vitro cellular uptake and subsequent cellular imaging. Multicolor images were obtained using laser confocal microscopy imaging of L929 and SW1736 cells incubated with BL-MECDs for 24 h. Fluorescent imaging of L929 cells showed all three colors at the respective wavelength, which indicates an active uptake of BL-MECDs into cells. The overlay image showed that BL-MECDs are distributed throughout the cells. Furthermore, almost all cells show fluorescent signals (Fig. 8 (a)). Similarly, Fluorescent Imaging of SW1736 cells also showed all three colors at the respective wavelength, which indicates an active uptake of BL-MECDs into cells. Similarly, all three colors were dominantly expressed in cancer cells as well as in normal fibroblast cells. The overlay image showed that BL-MECDs are distributed throughout the SW1736 cells. Furthermore, all cells show fluorescent signals (Fig. 8 (b)). As shown here, BL-MECDs emit multicolor than compared to other fluorescent labeling agents [60–62]. Thus, the synthesized BL-MECDs with a multi-range of excitation wavelength, low cellular toxicity, and strong multifluorescent emission, BL-MECDs can be a potential candidate for real-time cell imaging.

In summary, using betel leaves, BL-MECDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal reaction at the reaction times of 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, and 48 h. The physicochemical characterization of BL-MECDs was investigated carefully. The prepared BL-MECDs have a spherical shape (average diameter is 4.5 ± 0.5 nm) with functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups which are responsible for the hydrophilic nature of the surface. In addition, BL-MECDs showed strong excitation-dependent fluorescence emission. BL-MECDs prepared for 36 h showed enriched functional groups and the strongest fluorescence with an acceptable QY of 15% compared to other BL-MECDs prepared in 12 h, 24 h, and 48 h. Besides, BL-MECDs have excellent prolonged stability and photostability. Thus, BL-MECDs prepared for 36 h were used for bioimaging applications. The BL-MECDs showed low toxicity against L929 and SW1736 cells thus employed for cell imaging that showed the bright multicolor emission. As a result, BL-MECDs can be a potential candidate for real-time imaging of cells in vitro. The bright multicolor BL-MECDs can be a good candidate for monitoring live cells in vivo.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government MSIT (2021R1A2B5B02002436).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physe.2021.115010.
Animals or humans are not involved in this study. The details of the cell lines are included in the materials and methods.
